quiz_questions.question_text: What is the phase change from solid to liquid called?

options.text: Freezing
Sublimation
Condensation
Melting

quiz_questions.explanation: Melting is the transition from solid to liquid. It occurs when a solid absorbs enough energy to overcome the forces holding its particles in fixed positions, allowing them to move more freely.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the phase change from gas to liquid called?

options.text: Melting
Condensation
Evaporation
Deposition

quiz_questions.explanation: Condensation occurs when gas particles lose energy and slow down enough for intermolecular forces to pull them into the closer arrangement of a liquid. It is an exothermic process (releases energy).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which phase change goes directly from solid to gas without passing through the liquid phase?

options.text: Evaporation
Deposition
Sublimation
Melting

quiz_questions.explanation: Sublimation is the direct transition from solid to gas. A common example is dry ice (solid CO₂), which turns directly into carbon dioxide gas at normal atmospheric pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a phase change, what happens to the temperature of the substance?

options.text: It increases rapidly
It fluctuates randomly
It decreases rapidly
It remains constant

quiz_questions.explanation: During a phase change, all added or removed energy goes toward breaking or forming intermolecular forces — not changing temperature. This is why a heating curve shows flat plateaus at the melting and boiling points.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which phase change releases energy to the surroundings?

options.text: Freezing
Evaporation
Melting
Sublimation

quiz_questions.explanation: Freezing (liquid → solid) is exothermic — particles slow down and form stronger intermolecular bonds, releasing energy. Melting, evaporation, and sublimation all absorb energy (endothermic).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the phase change from liquid to gas called?

options.text: Freezing
Condensation
Vaporization
Deposition

quiz_questions.explanation: Vaporization is the transition from liquid to gas. It can occur as evaporation (at the surface, below boiling point) or boiling (throughout the liquid, at the boiling point). Both are endothermic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is deposition?

options.text: Solid changing to gas
Liquid changing to solid
Gas changing directly to solid without becoming liquid first
Gas changing to liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: Deposition is the reverse of sublimation — gas converts directly to solid without passing through the liquid phase. Frost forming on a cold window is a common example. It is an exothermic process.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which phase change requires the most energy input: melting or evaporation?

options.text: Evaporation — because all intermolecular forces must be overcome for particles to become gas
Neither requires energy — both are exothermic
Melting — because solid particles are harder to separate than liquid particles
They require exactly the same amount of energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation (or boiling) requires more energy than melting because particles must completely overcome all intermolecular forces to enter the gas phase. In melting, particles only loosen their fixed positions while staying close together.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between evaporation and boiling?

options.text: Evaporation occurs at the surface below the boiling point; boiling occurs throughout the liquid at the boiling point
Boiling only happens in water; evaporation happens in all liquids
Evaporation produces steam; boiling does not
There is no difference — they are the same process

quiz_questions.explanation: Both are forms of vaporization (liquid → gas), but evaporation occurs only at the liquid surface and can happen at any temperature. Boiling occurs throughout the entire liquid when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure at the boiling point.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a heating curve, flat horizontal sections (plateaus) represent:

options.text: Errors in the measurement equipment
Phase changes — energy is used to break intermolecular forces, not raise temperature
Temperature increases within a single phase
Periods when no heat is being added

quiz_questions.explanation: During a phase change, the temperature stays constant even though heat is being added. All the energy goes into breaking intermolecular forces (changing the arrangement of particles) rather than increasing kinetic energy (temperature).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'melting point'?

options.text: The temperature at which a substance decomposes
The temperature at which a liquid changes to a gas
The temperature at which a gas becomes plasma
The temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid at standard pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: The melting point is the specific temperature at which a solid's particles gain enough energy to break free from their fixed positions and transition to the liquid phase. At standard pressure, ice melts at 0°C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'endothermic' mean in the context of phase changes?

options.text: A process that absorbs energy from the surroundings
A process that creates new chemical bonds
A process that releases energy to the surroundings
A process that destroys energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Endothermic means 'heat in' — the process absorbs energy from the surroundings. Melting, evaporation, and sublimation are all endothermic because energy must be added to overcome intermolecular forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'exothermic' mean in the context of phase changes?

options.text: A process that releases energy to the surroundings
A process that only occurs at high temperatures
A process that absorbs energy from the surroundings
A process that breaks chemical bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Exothermic means 'heat out' — the process releases energy to the surroundings. Freezing, condensation, and deposition are exothermic because particles form stronger intermolecular bonds and release the stored energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'heat of fusion'?

options.text: The total thermal energy in a solid object
The energy required to change a substance from solid to liquid at its melting point
The energy required to change a substance from liquid to gas
The temperature at which two metals fuse together

quiz_questions.explanation: Heat of fusion (ΔHfus) is the amount of energy needed per gram (or mole) to convert a solid to a liquid at its melting point. For water, this is 334 J/g. The reverse process (freezing) releases the same amount of energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'heat of vaporization'?

options.text: The energy required to change a substance from liquid to gas at its boiling point
The energy required to change a substance from solid to liquid
The temperature at which a liquid begins to boil
The speed at which gas particles move

quiz_questions.explanation: Heat of vaporization (ΔHvap) is the energy needed per gram (or mole) to convert a liquid to gas at its boiling point. For water, it is 2,260 J/g — much larger than the heat of fusion because all intermolecular forces must be fully overcome.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'boiling point' mean?

options.text: The highest temperature a liquid can reach
The temperature at which a liquid's vapor pressure equals the atmospheric pressure above it
The temperature at which bubbles first appear in any liquid
The temperature at which a solid melts

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling point is the temperature at which the vapor pressure of a liquid equals the external atmospheric pressure, causing vaporization throughout the liquid (not just at the surface). At sea level, water boils at 100°C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'heating curve'?

options.text: A table listing boiling points of all elements
A measurement of how fast a substance heats up
A graph showing temperature vs. energy added, with plateaus at phase changes
A device used to heat liquids in a lab

quiz_questions.explanation: A heating curve plots temperature on the y-axis against energy (or time of heating) on the x-axis. Sloped sections show temperature rising within a single phase; flat plateaus show phase changes where temperature stays constant as energy goes into breaking intermolecular forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'vapor pressure' mean?

options.text: The temperature at which vapor turns back into liquid
The force needed to compress a gas into a liquid
The weight of water vapor in the atmosphere
The pressure exerted by the vapor above a liquid in a closed container at equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by a vapor in equilibrium with its liquid (or solid) in a closed container. It increases with temperature because more molecules have enough energy to escape into the gas phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'triple point' of a substance?

options.text: The temperature at which a substance boils three times faster
The point at which three different substances mix together
The specific temperature and pressure at which solid, liquid, and gas phases coexist in equilibrium
The third phase change in a heating curve

quiz_questions.explanation: The triple point is a unique set of temperature and pressure conditions where all three phases of a substance exist simultaneously in equilibrium. For water, the triple point is 0.01°C at 611.73 Pa.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'condensation point' mean?

options.text: The temperature at which dew forms, which varies based on humidity
The point at which a liquid becomes saturated
The pressure at which a gas becomes a solid
The temperature at which a gas changes to a liquid — numerically equal to the boiling point

quiz_questions.explanation: The condensation point and the boiling point are the same temperature for a given substance at the same pressure. The process is just reversed: boiling adds energy (endothermic), while condensation releases energy (exothermic).

quiz_questions.question_text: On a cold morning, a student sees frost forming on a car windshield even though it did not rain overnight. Which phase change produced the frost?

options.text: Sublimation — ice from the ground rose up to the windshield
Freezing — liquid water on the windshield froze overnight
Deposition — water vapor in the air changed directly to solid ice on the cold surface
Condensation — water vapor became liquid droplets that then froze

quiz_questions.explanation: Frost forms through deposition — water vapor (gas) in the air contacts the cold windshield surface and transitions directly to solid ice crystals without first becoming liquid. This is the reverse of sublimation and is exothermic.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pot of water is boiling on a stove. A student measures the temperature and finds it stays at 100°C even as more heat is added. Why doesn't the temperature increase?

options.text: During the phase change, all added energy goes into breaking intermolecular forces rather than raising temperature
The stove stopped producing heat once the water reached 100°C
Water cannot absorb any more energy after reaching 100°C
The thermometer is broken and cannot read above 100°C

quiz_questions.explanation: At the boiling point, energy is used to overcome intermolecular forces and convert liquid molecules to gas — this is the heat of vaporization. Temperature only rises again once all the liquid has been converted to steam.

quiz_questions.question_text: Wet clothes hung on a clothesline dry even on a cool day when the temperature is well below 100°C. What process is responsible?

options.text: Evaporation — surface water molecules with enough kinetic energy escape into the air
Condensation — moisture leaves the clothes and enters the air as liquid droplets
Boiling — the water boils at lower temperatures outdoors
Sublimation — the water turns directly from liquid to solid

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation occurs at any temperature when surface molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome intermolecular forces and escape into the gas phase. Wind and low humidity speed up evaporation by removing vapor from the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hiker at high altitude (5,000 m) tries to cook pasta but notices the water boils at about 83°C instead of 100°C. The pasta takes much longer to cook. Why?

options.text: Lower atmospheric pressure lowers the boiling point, so the water is less hot and cooks food more slowly
The water contains fewer minerals at high altitude
UV radiation at high altitude prevents water from reaching 100°C
The thinner air makes the flame on the stove weaker

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure. At 5,000 m, atmospheric pressure is much lower, so water boils at ~83°C. Since the water is cooler, chemical reactions in cooking (which depend on temperature) proceed more slowly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places mothballs in a closet. Over several weeks, the mothballs shrink and eventually disappear, but no liquid residue is found. What phase change occurred?

options.text: Decomposition — the mothballs broke down into elements
Sublimation — the solid naphthalene changed directly to gas
Evaporation — the mothballs melted and then evaporated
Condensation — the mothball gas became liquid and drained away

quiz_questions.explanation: Mothballs are made of naphthalene (or paradichlorobenzene), which sublimes at room temperature — the solid slowly converts directly to gas without becoming liquid. This is why you can smell them (the gas reaches your nose) but never see a puddle.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a humid summer day, water droplets form on the outside of a cold glass of iced tea. Which phase change causes these droplets to appear?

options.text: Evaporation — the tea inside evaporates through the glass
Condensation — water vapor in the warm air cools on the cold glass surface and becomes liquid
Melting — ice from inside the glass leaks through
Deposition — water vapor freezes directly onto the glass

quiz_questions.explanation: Warm, humid air contains water vapor. When this air contacts the cold glass surface, the vapor molecules lose kinetic energy and slow down enough for intermolecular forces to pull them together into liquid droplets — this is condensation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pressure cooker seals steam inside, raising the internal pressure above normal atmospheric pressure. How does this affect the boiling point of the water inside?

options.text: The water cannot boil at all under high pressure
The boiling point stays the same — pressure does not affect phase changes
The boiling point decreases — pressure forces water to boil faster
The boiling point increases — higher pressure requires more energy for molecules to escape into the gas phase

quiz_questions.explanation: Increased pressure raises the boiling point because more energy is needed for liquid molecules to push against the higher external pressure and escape into the gas phase. A pressure cooker reaches ~120°C, cooking food faster than normal boiling.

quiz_questions.question_text: After stepping out of a swimming pool, a person feels cold even though the air temperature is 30°C. Which phase change is responsible for the cold sensation?

options.text: Condensation — the air condenses moisture onto the cold skin
Sublimation — the water skips the liquid phase and becomes gas
Evaporation — water on the skin absorbs body heat as it changes from liquid to gas
Freezing — the pool water freezes on the skin surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation is endothermic — it absorbs heat energy from the surroundings (your skin). As water molecules on your skin gain enough energy to escape into the gas phase, they take that thermal energy with them, leaving your skin feeling cold.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist examines a phase diagram and notices that CO₂ has no liquid phase at normal atmospheric pressure (1 atm). What does this tell us about dry ice at room conditions?

options.text: CO₂ goes directly from gas to plasma at room temperature
Liquid CO₂ exists at 1 atm but evaporates too quickly to see
At 1 atm, solid CO₂ can only sublime — it cannot melt into a liquid
CO₂ has no solid phase at any pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: The phase diagram for CO₂ shows that the triple point is at 5.11 atm. Below this pressure, the liquid phase does not exist, so solid CO₂ at 1 atm can only sublimate directly to gas. Liquid CO₂ requires pressures above 5.11 atm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A freeze-drying machine first freezes food solid, then lowers the pressure and gently heats it so the ice disappears without the food ever becoming wet. Which sequence of phase changes makes this possible?

options.text: Freezing (liquid → solid), then sublimation (solid → gas) under low pressure
Condensation (gas → liquid), then deposition (gas → solid)
Freezing (liquid → solid), then melting (solid → liquid), then evaporation (liquid → gas)
Evaporation (liquid → gas), then deposition (gas → solid)

quiz_questions.explanation: Freeze-drying works by first freezing the food, then reducing pressure so the frozen water sublimes directly from solid to gas — bypassing the liquid phase entirely. This preserves the food's structure because it never becomes wet or soggy during the drying process.
